cards.definition: the situation or problem that a designer is responding to — Eduqas NEA starts with a design context issued by the board
the Eduqas D&T coursework component — worth 50% of the GCSE. Students design and make a product in response to a brief
a cycle of designing, making, testing, and improving — used throughout the NEA
the requirements of the intended user — research into user needs informs the design specification
first-hand data gathered directly — e.g. questionnaires, interviews, observations of users
existing data gathered by others — e.g. websites, books, existing products (analysis)
a detailed list of criteria the final product must meet — based on research findings
a physical model used to test and evaluate a design idea — helps identify improvements before final making
testing the final product against each point in the design specification — identifies how well it meets user needs
understanding a product as a system with inputs, processes, and outputs — used in electronic and mechanical design
evaluating the environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction to end of life — used to justify sustainable design decisions
designing products to have a limited lifespan so consumers replace them — criticised for creating unnecessary waste
designing and making in ways that meet current needs without damaging the ability of future generations to meet their needs
a framework for sustainable design: Rethink, Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle
materials that will eventually run out — e.g. oil, metals, hardwoods. Designers should minimise their use
materials that can be replenished naturally — e.g. timber from managed forests, plant-based plastics
ensuring producers in developing countries receive fair wages and safe conditions — an ethical consideration in material sourcing
the total greenhouse gas emissions caused by a product or activity — reducing carbon footprint is a key sustainability goal
creatively reusing waste materials to create products of higher value — a sustainable making strategy
designing products so components can be easily separated at end of life for recycling or reuse
